title: OSP Endpoint Schema
description: This schema defines the user interface to the osp (OpenStack Platform) endpoint.  The osp endpoint deploys engines as virtual machine instances in an OpenStack environment.

type.description: The type parameter is how rickshaw-run knows what endpoint code to execute and assign a given endpoint configuration.

controller-ip.description: This parameter is used to provide an explicit IP address to the engines for use when connecting to the controller.  If not specified, the address is automatically derived from the 'host' parameter.  In environments with complex networking configurations it may be necessary for the user to provide this value explicitly.

host.description: The hostname or IP address of the OpenStack controller where control plane commands (such as the OpenStack CLI) can be executed.

user.description: The username used to log in to the OpenStack controller host via SSH.

userenv.description: This parameter defines the userenv that is to be assigned to all engines on this endpoint.  The userenv specifies the base container image used to build the engine images.

unique-project.description: When set, an isolated OpenStack project is created for this run to avoid resource conflicts with other concurrent runs or existing workloads.

client.description: This parameter controls which client engines belong to this endpoint.  The 'number-lists' definition below explains what the available formats are.

server.description: This parameter controls which server engines belong to this endpoint.  The 'number-lists' definition below explains what the available formats are.

config.description: A list of configuration blocks.  Each block consists of the targets (ie. engines) it applies to and the settings that should be applied to those targets.

items.description: Each item in this list defines one configuration block with a target scope and associated settings.

targets.description: This parameter defines what target engines this configuration block applies to.  There are two different versions of this parameter to choose from.

anyOf.description: This version of the 'targets' parameter defines a list of one or more targets that this configuration block will explicitly apply to.

items.description: Each item in the targets list will include the role and the IDs for that role that this configuration block should apply to.

role.description: The engine role being targeted by this configuration block.

ids.description: This parameter defines which engines with the specified role are being targeted.  The 'number-lists' definition below explains what the available formats are.

anyOf.description: This version of the 'targets' parameter uses a keyword to define the scope.  'all' applies to every engine and 'default' applies to any engine that does not have a block that specifically targets it.

settings.description: This object contains one or more properties that will be applied to the associated target engines.
The properties defined here can be assigned as either the default or explicitly targeted engine configuration options.

number-lists.description: This definition defines multiple ways to describe a list of numbers.  There are three different definitions to choose from.

anyOf.description: This version of the definition is a + separated list of values and/or ranges of numbers.  Since this definition can contain both numbers and symbols it is represented as a string.  Some examples are '1-5+6-10', '1+3+5', or '1-3+5+7-9'.
This version of the definition is a single integer value.  Some examples are 1, 5, or 10.
This version of the definition is a list of one or more values.  Since this is a list there is no need for the individual items to support multiple ranges or values.

items.description: This definition defines the ability to specify a range or an individual value.

anyOf.description: This version of the definition defines a range.  Since it contains a special character it is represented as a string.  Some examples include '1-5', '3-7', or '1-10'.
This version of the definition defines a single integer value.  Some examples are 3, 6, or 9.
